claim: Preparation route (external topical application vs internal ingestion) does NOT explain the _.oii vowel-pattern fire rate on plant folios. External-classified folios (n=24) and internal-classified folios (n=54) have essentially identical mean _.oii rates (0.561% vs 0.557%, ratio 1.01x). One-tailed Welch's t-test p = 0.494, Cohen's d = 0.003, Mann-Whitney U p = 0.341. Bootstrap 95% CI on difference [-0.0046, +0.0048] straddles zero.
evidence_for: 24 external folios vs 54 internal folios, adequate sample size
95% CI on difference well-centered on zero, not near exclusion
evidence_against: Classification is keyword-heuristic from Sherwood notes, not expert ethnobotanical labels
Some plants have both internal and external uses (3 were flagged MIXED)
42 folios were UNKNOWN (no keywords matched) and were excluded

tests_run.details: p = 0.494 (far above 0.05). d = 0.003. Hypothesis refuted cleanly.
tests_run.confidence_change: initial 0.90 - high confidence the null is real

tests_remaining: Follow-up hypothesis: psychoactive plants (narcotic/hallucinogenic/soporific) fire _.oii more than non-psychoactive. Observed post-hoc in the toxic cross-check (Paris, Rhododendron, Nymphaea all psychoactive + firing; Atropa, Mandragora, Ricinus non-firing despite also psychoactive — counter-signal). Needs explicit pre-registered formulation before testing.
reasoning: Brain-V has now attempted two content-encoding sub-hypotheses for the H-BV-PLANT-01 _.oii enrichment: toxicity (p=0.060, marginal) and external-application (p=0.494, null). The field of candidate explanations is narrower by one more. The bimodality of toxic _.oii firing remains unexplained; a post-hoc observation suggests psychoactive plants may be the discriminator, but this must be pre-registered and tested separately.
